fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

PARKER J:

INTRODUCTION

The issues before the Court for determination relate to:
(a) liability for, and the quantum of, interest payable on sums to be paid by the respondent husband, MR CATLIN (“the husband”) to the applicant wife, MS CATLIN (“the wife”) pursuant to final property orders made by consent between them pursuant to section 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) on 22 July 2016 (“the consent orders”) up to 8 December 2025; and

(b) costs payable by the husband to the wife arising from enforcement proceedings with respect to the consent orders.

BACKGROUND

The wife was born in 1969 and is aged 56. The husband was born in 1969 and is also aged 56.
The husband and the wife (collectively “the parties”) were married in 1994 and commenced cohabitation upon their marriage. They separated on 12 April 2013.
There are three children of the marriage, all of whom are now adults.
The wife has at all material times been employed in the financial services profession. The husband also holds financial services qualifications and is engaged by B Pty Ltd.
During their marriage, the parties established a self-managed superannuation fund known as the Catlin Superannuation Fund (“the superannuation fund”). Both parties are members of the superannuation fund and directors of its corporate trustee.
During the parties’ marriage, the husband, together with Mr Q and Mr T, formed a partnership through which a practice known as S Pty Ltd was operated (“[S Partnership]”). The partners of S Partnership were the husband in his own capacity and as director of O Pty Ltd (as trustee for the O Trust), Mr Q in his own capacity and as director of P Pty Ltd (as trustee for the P Trust), and Mr T in his own capacity and as director of R Pty Ltd (as trustee for R Trust).
In 2014, approximately a year after the parties separated, the husband exited the S Partnership and a partnership dispute arose.
In November 2014, the husband and the other S Pty Ltd partners participated in a mediation and entered into a Heads of Agreement (“the Heads of Agreement”) which provided, inter alia, for the distribution of work in progress (“WIP”) between them. Although the acronym “WIP” usually refers only to unbilled fees, it has been used throughout these proceedings, and is therefore used in these reasons, to refer collectively to unbilled, billed and collected fees associated with the S Partnership.
The relevant terms of the Heads of Agreement as follows:
Notwithstanding those matters set out in paragraphs 2 to 7 above, all WIP that is realised and paid into [Mr Catlin’s] NAB WIP account and all WIP that is realised and paid into [Mr Q’s] and [Mr T’s] NAB WIP account is realised and paid on behalf of the old partnership. The parties agree that there will be monthly reporting to each of them of all WIP that is realised and a reconciliation as between [Mr Catlin], [Mr Q] and [Mr T] as at 31 December 2016 or at such other times as the parties may agree.
The reconciliation is on the basis of one-third each and old partnership accounts will be struck on that basis as at 31 December 2016. The parties will agree any adjustments by 30 April 2017 or such other time as agreed to by the parties. Thereafter those accounts will be further reconciled bi annually, and agreed within 60 days of each six month period. Any negative adjustments will be paid by that party.
The parties were engaged in litigation with respect to financial matters (and later parenting matters) from March 2014. On 22 July 2016, in the days leading up to a trial listing in the Family Court of Australia (as this court was then known), the parties entered into consent orders resolving all of the proceedings between them. The consent orders relevantly provided as follows:
The husband shall pay or cause to be paid to the wife 60% of the net funds received by the husband in his own capacity and/or as a director of [O Pty Ltd] from the partnership [S Pty Ltd] pursuant to clause 10 of the Heads of Agreement dated 07/11/2014 between the husband and his former [S Pty Ltd] partners or otherwise, after the payment of the following:
17.1 Such sum as is required to pay the husband's one third share of the NAB liability in the name [O Pty Ltd] (noting that the total debt is approximately $420,000) until such time as the debt is paid in full;

17.2 Any taxation liability and GST payable by the husband or by entities in which he has an interest, following receipt of income from [S Pty Ltd];

17.3 Any outstanding leave or other entitlements for staff owing by the husband to [S Pty Ltd];

17.4 Any other partnership liabilities;

17.5 with the balance to be paid to the husband provided that should there be any shortfall in respect of the funds received and monies paid or to be paid pursuant to this Order such shortfall be borne by the husband and the husband do indemnify and keep indemnified the wife in relation to same.
That the husband do cause the wife to be provided by email within three (3) months with all “Form 524 Presentation of accounts and statement” lodged with ASIC [the Australian Securities and Investments Commission] in relation to [S Pty Ltd] since the husband left the partnership and within one month of any other such forms that are hereinafter lodged.
...

SUPERANNUATION
That in accordance withs 90MT(1)(a) of the Family Law Act whenever a splittable payment becomes payable from the superannuation interest held by the wife in the [Catlin Superannuation Fund] (“The Fund”): -
36.1 The Trustee shall do all such acts matters and things so as to pay to the wife the entitlement calculated in accord with Part 6 of the Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2001 using a base amount of ONE HUNDRED AND THIRTY THOUSAND SIX HUNDRED AND TWENTY THREE DOLLARS ($130,623) plus 6% calculated from 18 November 2015 to the date of the rollover referred to in paragraph 36.7.3 less the amount of the wife's member entitlements as at 30/06/2018; and

36.2 There shall be a corresponding reduction in the entitlement that the husband would have had in The Fund but for these Orders;

36.3 That Order 36 shall have effect from the operative time;

36.4 The operative time shall be on 30/06/2018;

36.5 That this Order binds and shall bind the Trustees of The Fund;

36.6 Liberty to apply generally is reserved to the Trustee; and

36.7 Forthwith upon the preceding orders 36.1 to 36.5 having been carried out in full:
36.7.1 the wife shall resign as a trustee of The Fund;

36.7.2 the wife shall if necessary resign as a director and transfer all her shares in [K Pty Ltd] (the trustee of The Fund) and [K2 Pty Ltd] to the husband;

36.7.3 the wife shall as soon as practicable after 30/06/2018 then rollover her member entitlements into a Superannuation Fund of her choice;

36.7.4 the husband and wife will sign all such documents and do all such things as are necessary including but not limited to selling or encumbering any asset or calling in any liability to facilitate the said rollover; and

36.7.5 the husband including in his capacity as trustee of The Fund shall thereafter fully indemnify the wife in relation to all claims against The Fund.
36.8 Thereafter each party shall retain free of any further claim or demand or right or entitlement of the other their respective superannuation entitlements.
The consent orders also provided, inter alia, for the wife’s interest in the parties’ former matrimonial home at F Street, Suburb G to be transferred to the husband.
As is apparent from their terms, at the time the consent orders were entered into, the quantum of the WIP which might ultimately be recovered by the husband from S Pty Ltd was unknown. At the time of the making of the consent orders, the parties had limited other assets.
Following the making of the consent orders, no funds were paid by the husband to the wife pursuant to order 17 despite the husband having ultimately received over $1.6 million in fees (ie collected WIP) following his exit from S Pty Ltd. The superannuation split provided for at paragraph 36 was not implemented. The former matrimonial home was transferred into the sole name of the husband and a 50 per cent interest in that property was subsequently transferred by him to his current wife, MS CARTER, for no consideration.

ENFORCEMENT AND RELATED PROCEEDINGS

Enforcement application

On 11 July 2023, the wife filed an Application - Enforcement (“enforcement application”). That application sought, inter alia, that the husband provide disclosure, that there be a declaration as to the amount payable by the husband pursuant to order 17 of the consent orders, the sale of the Suburb G property so as to facilitate payment to the wife of the amount owing to her, and orders directed to implementation of the superannuation split provided for at paragraph 36 of the consent orders.
On 22 December 2023, the husband filed a Response seeking that the enforcement application be dismissed.
On 31 May 2024, an order was made by consent providing for a separate hearing and decision with respect to specified aspects of the relief sought in the enforcement application, namely determination of the amounts (if any) owing to the wife pursuant to orders 17 and 36 of the consent orders.
That separate hearing proceeded before Berman J on 24 and 25 July 2024 and was then adjourned part heard at the wife’s request following the husband’s evidence, on the basis of a foreshadowed application pursuant to section 79A of the Act, which was not ultimately filed.
On 16 August 2024, the wife filed an Initiating Application in which she sought a variation of orders 17 and 18 of the final orders pursuant to rule 10.13(1)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) on the basis that those orders did not reflect the intention of the Court. This was pressed before Berman J as an alternative position only, the wife’s primary position remaining the orders sought in the enforcement application.
The hearing before Berman J continued on 9 and 11 December 2024.
As set out above, order 17 of the consent orders made reference to clause 10 of the Heads of Agreement. The S Pty Ltd partners did not ultimately implement that clause, and no ongoing reconciliations of the fees collected by the S Pty Ltd partners pursuant to their respective WIP accounts took place after the date upon which the consent orders were made.
The husband’s position before Berman J was effectively that the reconciliation provided for in the Heads of Agreement was a condition precedent to the emergence of any liability owed by him to make a payment to the wife pursuant to order 17 of the consent orders. He argued that because there had been no such reconciliation, no such liability had arisen.
In relation to the superannuation split provided for at order 36 of the consent orders, the husband contended before Berman J that the calculation of the wife’s entitlement should be determined using an amount of $130,623 together with 6 per cent interest calculated from 18 November 2015 to the date of the rollover, less the wife’s member entitlement as at 30 June 2018. The wife disputed the suggestion that the order required the deduction of her member balance.

Judgment of Berman J

Justice Berman delivered judgment on 7 February 2025.[1] His Honour rejected the husband’s interpretation of his obligations pursuant to order 17 and held that it was “not a credible argument” that the husband’s obligation to the wife was limited to any adjustment that occurred upon a reconciliation, thereby ignoring the S Pty Ltd WIP otherwise collected and retained by the husband.[2]
His Honour further held that there was no basis for what was ultimately established to be a substantial sum to have been excluded from the identified assets of the parties, particularly in circumstances where any WIP to be received by the husband was recognised at the time of the making of the consent orders as being the only likely asset of value.[3]
In summary, his Honour determined that the effect of order 17 was not that the husband was required to pay to the wife 60 per cent of a “reconciliation” or “adjustment” between the S Pty Ltd partners, but 60 per cent of the total net WIP (less the specified liabilities) ultimately received by him.
His Honour accepted that the husband had received $1,624,044.48 (exclusive of GST) between April 2014 and June 2020.
With respect to superannuation, his Honour preferred the interpretation promoted by the wife, namely that her member balance was not to be deducted from her entitlements.
By orders made on 7 February 2025, Berman J declared that, pursuant to the consent orders, the husband was liable to pay to the wife the sum of $724,426.49; and that the wife’s entitlement in the superannuation fund, inclusive of her member entitlement as at 30 June 2018, was fixed at $130,623. His Honour’s orders otherwise dismissed the Initiating Application filed on 16 August 2024 and listed the enforcement application for further directions.

Appeal

On 28 February 2025, the husband appealed from the orders of Berman J.
On 5 June 2025, being the date upon which the appeal was listed for hearing, the Full Court of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) (“the Full Court”), comprised of Hartnett, Jarrett and Christie JJ, ordered that the husband not be heard on his Notice of Appeal and that the Notice of Appeal be dismissed with costs.
The reasons for that decision were delivered on 24 June 2025.[4] In summary, the husband’s appeal was dismissed in accordance with the principles in Hadkinson v Hadkinson,[5] the Full Court having exercised its discretion to decline to hear the husband’s appeal in circumstances in which he stood in contempt of court. The details of the husband’s relevant non-compliance with the various obligations imposed on him by the Rules and by orders of the Court are discussed below.
On 3 July 2025, the husband filed an application for special leave to appeal to the High Court of Australia (“the High Court”). That application was dismissed with costs on 9 October 2025.

Orders made 8 December 2025

The remaining relief as sought in the enforcement application was listed for final hearing before me on 8 December 2025.
On 2 September 2025, the wife filed a further Initiating Application seeking, inter alia, to set aside the transfer of the 50 per cent interest in the former matrimonial home to the husband’s current wife pursuant to section 106B of the Act, and orders as to disclosure.
On 11 July 2025, the husband’s current wife was joined as second respondent.
On 18 November 2025, the wife filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking to proceed with the extant applications on an undefended basis, citing further and ongoing non-compliance on the part of the husband (and the second respondent).
On 8 December 2025, at which time the balance of the enforcement application, the Initiating Application filed on 2 September 2025 and the Application in a Proceeding filed on 18 November 2025 were all listed for hearing, orders were made by consent resolving all extant aspects of the dispute between the parties (save for those determined by way of these reasons) in the following terms:
UPON NOTING THAT:
The parties agree that the outstanding issues of interest and costs are to be determined on the papers without a further oral hearing.

THE COURT ORDERS BY CONSENT THAT:

Sale of [F Street] [Suburb G]
The First and Second Respondents shall use their best endeavours to secure the sale of the property situated at [F Street], [Suburb G] in the State of South Australia, being the whole of the land described in the Certificate of Title Volume [...] Folio [...] ('the Property') as soon as reasonably practicable.
The First and Second Respondents do all acts and things and sign all necessary documents to list the Property for sale by private treaty or private auction and for that purpose the following shall apply:
2.1 Within 14 days of the date of this order, the First and Second Respondents shall engage such real estate agent ('Agent') as is determined by them and shall provide a copy of these orders to them;

2.2 Within 30 days of the date of this order, the First and Second Respondents shall sign a Sales Agency Agreement with the Agent, subject to any sale campaign commencing as soon as reasonably practicable after the works referred to at paragraph 2.3 are complete;

2.3 The First and Second Respondents are authorised to undertake such works ('Works') to the Property as recommended by the Agent as soon as reasonably practicable including but not limited to:
2.3.1 Repairs to the retaining wall adjacent to the driveway at the Property;

2.3.2 Repairs to the balustrades & balcony on the first floor of the Property;

2.3.3 Painting the interior and exterior of the Property;

2.3.4 Replacing the rear exterior decking and;

2.3.5 Gardening and landscaping.
2.4 The First and Second Respondents are authorised to obtaining funding to a cap of $150,000 for the Works from their existing mortgagee or from a third party ('the Funder') agreed in writing with the Applicant ('the Approved Works') on the basis that the Funder:
2.4.1 shall be entitled to lodge a caveat ('the Caveat') over the Property;

2.4.2 shall be paid its fees, and interest, and amounts paid with respect to the Approved Works,
at settlement of the sale of the Property.

2.5 The Property shall be marketed for sale as soon as reasonably practicable after completion of the Approved Works. The Property shall be marketed for sale in accordance with the Agent’s recommendations (in writing or otherwise) including but not limited to in relation to any advertising, marketing, works, improvements or remedial works to prepare the property for sale, the method of sale, the marketing campaign save for as provided for in these orders;

2.6 The Property shall be marketed for sale for a sale period of four (4) weeks unless otherwise agreed between the Applicant and the First and Second Respondents in writing or in accordance with the recommendations of the Agent;

2.7 The listing and reserve price shall be based upon the advice and recommendations of the Agent;

2.8 The parties are to co-operate in every way with the Agent in relation to the marketing of the Property for sale including making the key readily available, allowing inspection of the Property at all times reasonably requested by the agent and ensuring that the Property is clean, neat and in good order at the time of inspection by any prospective buyer;

2.9 The Agents fees, including any amounts for advertising and sale expenses shall be paid at settlement of the sale of the Property;

2.10 The First and Second Respondents shall instruct and authorise the Agent;

2.11 The First and Second Respondents shall do all such acts and sign all such documents necessary to complete the sale of the property including signing the Contract of Sale and all other transfer documentation forthwith upon its submission to them by the Agent or conveyancer or solicitor;

2.12 The First and Second Respondents shall provide to any conveyancer or solicitor engaged in connection with the sale of the property a copy of these orders; and

2.13 In the event that the Property has not been sold pursuant to these orders by 30 April 2026 the Applicant is at liberty to apply to the Court for further orders in relation to the manner and timing of sale of the property.
The Applicant, First Respondent and Second Respondent shall receive all communications and correspondence from the Agent notwithstanding the property is owned by the First and Second Respondents to the exclusion of the Applicant, and this order stands as authority from the First and Second Respondents for the Agent to provide all communications and correspondence to the Applicant during the sale process.
Except where the sale of the Property proceeds by way of auction, the First and Second Respondents shall instruct and authorise the Agent to notify the Applicant, in writing by email to an address notified to the Agent in accordance with this order:

4.1 Of each offer received for the Purchase of the Property, subject to such offers being above the listing or reserve price; and

4.2 Where the First and Second Respondents intend to accept an offer for the sale and purchase of the Property, the First and Second Respondents’ intention to accept an offer made for the purchase of the Property, subject to the offer being above the listing or reserve price.
Upon the sale of the Property, the proceeds of sale shall be paid in the following manner and in order of priority:

5.1 To discharge any registered mortgage/s against the Property;

5.2 To discharge the caveat, or other security, registered against the Property by the Funder;

5.3 Any other encumbrance affecting the property including rates and taxes;

5.4 To meet all reasonable costs of sale, including payment of the agent’s commission and advertising or other expenses, if any, payable on the sale;

5.5 Payment of the conveyancing costs and outlays relating to the sale;

5.6 The balance thereafter to be divided as follows:
5.6.1 Payment of any amount due to the Applicant by order of this Court to be paid direct to a bank account nominated in writing by the Applicant including, for the avoidance of doubt:
(a) amounts due pursuant to the orders of the Family Court of Australia of 22 July 2016 and the orders of this Court of 7 February 2025;

(b) interest accrued on the amounts referred to in paragraph 5.6.1(a) from 8 December 2025 until the date of payment such interest to be calculated in accordance with the Rules;

(c) in respect to the period to 8 December 2025 any amount of interest determined by the Court to be payable;

(d) any costs of these proceedings as determined by the Court; and

(e) any obligation the First Respondent has to the Applicant with respect to the orders for costs made by the Full Court of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) and/or the High Court of Australia (the latter in the sum of $13,460.33) together with any amount by way of interest on the costs of those proceedings due under the relevant rules of court;

5.6.2 Payment to the [Catlin Superannuation Fund] (Fund) by way of contribution from the First Respondent and/or Second Respondent in a sum equal to the Applicant’s interest in that Fund (inclusive of any amount that may be determined by the Court for interest); and

5.6.3 The balance to the First and Second Respondents to be paid by way direct to their nominated account.
Interim use of the property
Pending the sale of the Property pursuant to these orders:
6.1 Except where otherwise expressly provided by these orders, the First and Second Respondents are restrained by injunction from encumbering the property without the prior written consent of the Applicant save for funding the costs of the remedial works or improvements as recommended by the Agent or as agreed between the parties in writing or provided for in these orders;

6.2 The First and Second Respondents are responsible for and shall cause to be repaired any damage to the Property or undertake remedial works or improvements as recommended by the Agent;

6.3 The First and Second Respondents are restrained by injunction from giving any notice or seeking or consenting to any order that affects the Property;

6.4 Both parties are restrained by injunction from making any agreement affecting the Property which binds the transferee to perform;

6.5 The First and Second Respondents are responsible for the day-to-day maintenance and care of the Property; and

6.6 The First and Second Respondents are directed to maintain, at their sole cost, building insurance for the Property at the current value and provide to the other a certificate of currency evidencing same within 2 business days of any request being made.
Superannuation
Within 28 days of the payment referred to in paragraph 5.6.2 above:
7.1 the Applicant will nominate a Superannuation Fund for the rollover of her member entitlements in the Fund; and

7.2 the First and the Second Respondent shall cause the trustee of the Fund to comply with paragraph 36 of the orders of 22 July 2016 in respect of the Applicant’s member entitlements in the Fund (inclusive of any amount agreed or determined by the Court in respect of interest) and to the extent necessary the Applicant will cooperate with the First Respondent and the Trustee in that regard.
Party solely liable for expenses of the property
Pending the sale of the Property pursuant to these orders the First and Second Respondents are solely liable for and indemnify the Applicant against the following outgoings in and about the Property:
8.1 Instalments and money due under any mortgage;

8.2 Rates and water;

8.3 Any land tax assessed;

8.4 The cost of all reasonably necessary repairs and maintenance to the structures and or buildings located on the real property;

8.5 The premiums for the continuation of current insurance policies on the house and contents located on the real property;

8.6 Utility expenses including but not limited to gas, electricity and telephone usage.
Removal of injunction
The orders at paragraph 5 of the orders made by Judge Cole on 15 December 2023 in action number (P)ADC748/2014 be discharged.
The orders at paragraph 1(a) and (b) of the orders made by the Honourable Justice Parker on 12 June 2025 are hereby discharged.
The order at paragraph 1(d) of the orders made by the Honourable Justice Parker on 12 June 2025 is varied such that the Second Respondent may be appointed a director of the companies.

12. Paragraph 36.7 of the orders of 22 July 2016 are varied such that:
12.1 first, the wife shall rollover her member entitlements (inclusive of any amount of interest determined by the Court) as required by paragraph 36.7.3 and the husband and wife will facilitate said rollover as required by paragraph 36.7.4;

12.2 second, and only upon completion of the rollover of the wife’s entitlements, the wife shall resign as a trustee of the Fund as required by paragraph 36.7.1 and resign as a director and shareholder of the trustee companies as required by paragraph 36.7.2; and

12.3 third, the husband including as a trustee of the Fund will indemnify the wife as required by paragraph 36.7.5.
In the event that the Second Respondent is a trustee of the Fund or a director or shareholder of the trustee companies of the Fund (that is, [K Pty Ltd] and/or [K2 Pty Ltd]) at the time that the wife seeks to rollover her member entitlements the Second Respondent will sign all such documents and do all such things as are necessary including but not limited to selling or encumbering any asset or calling in any liability to facilitate the said rollover.
The order at paragraph 1(e) of the orders made by the Honourable Justice Parker on 12 June 2025 is hereby varied so that the real property owned by the companies may be mortgaged, encumbered or otherwise proffered as security solely to enable the Respondents to obtain funding in accordance with paragraph 2.4 of this order.
Upon the payment of the proceeds of sale in accordance with paragraph 5 above, the orders made by the Honourable Justice Parker on 12 June 2025 otherwise be discharged in their entirety.
THE COURT FURTHER ORDERS THAT:
Paragraphs 9-11, 13 and 14 of the Enforcement Application filed by the Wife on 11 July 2023 are withdrawn with a right of reinstatement in the event of further non-compliance with orders of the Court.
All parties have liberty to apply with respect to the implementation of these orders.
The applicant file and serve written submissions with respect to the outstanding issues of interest and costs by no later than the close of business on 9 December 2025.
The first respondent (and, should she choose to do so, the second respondent) file and serve written submissions with respect to the outstanding issues of interest and costs by no later than the close of business on 16 December 2025.
Any submissions in reply are to be filed no later than the close of business on 19 December 2025.
Following the making of those orders, as envisaged by their terms, the husband and the wife each filed written submissions with respect to the questions of interest and costs, being the remaining extant issues. The wife’s reply submissions were filed late, but upon notice being given to the parties and no objection being raised, the operation of rule 1.33 of the Rules was dispensed with in this regard and reliance upon those submissions was permitted. The second respondent did not participate in the applications with respect to interest and costs and no further orders were sought against her.

HUSBAND’S NON-COMPLIANCE WITH ORDERS AND OBLIGATIONS

The obligations pertaining to disclosure to which parties to financial proceedings under the Act are subject set out clearly in rules 6.01, 6.03 and 6.06 of the Rules,[6] which relevantly provide as follows:
6.01 General duty of disclosure
(1) Subject to subrule (4), each party to a proceeding has a duty to the court and to each other party to give full and frank disclosure of all information relevant to the proceeding, in a timely manner.
Note: The proceedings to which the duty of disclosure applies include both parenting proceedings and financial or property proceedings. For the duty of disclosure in proceedings relating to financial or property matters, see subsections 71B(1) and 90RI(1) of the Family Law Act. Failure to comply with the duty may result in the court excluding evidence that is not disclosed or imposing a consequence, including punishment for contempt of court.
(2) The duty of disclosure applies from the start of the proceeding and continues until the proceeding is finalised.
Note: Parties are also expected to comply with the duty of disclosure when complying with the pre‑action procedures. For the duty of disclosure while preparing for a proceeding relating to financial or property matters, see subsections 71B(5) and 90RI(5) of the Family Law Act.
...

6.03 Duty of disclosure—documents

The duty of disclosure applies to each document that:
(a) is or has been in the possession, or under the control, of the party disclosing the document; and
(b) is relevant to an issue in the proceeding.

Note 1: In particular types of proceedings, practice directions may specify the documents that must be disclosed in those proceedings. See also rules 6.05 and 6.06.

Note 2: A document, or part of a document, must not be disclosed if the court has made an order under subsection 102BD(1) of the Family Law Act in relation to the document or part of the document.
6.06 Duty of disclosure—financial or property proceedings
(1) Subrules (2) to (8) do not apply to a party to a financial or property proceeding who is not a party to the marriage or de facto relationship to which the application relates, except to the extent that the party’s financial circumstances are relevant to the issues in dispute.

(2) A party to a financial or property proceeding must make full and frank disclosure of the party’s financial circumstances, including the following:

(a) the party’s earnings, including income that is paid or assigned to another party, person or legal entity;
(b) any vested or contingent interest in property;
(c) any vested or contingent interest in property owned by a legal entity that is fully or partially owned or controlled by a party;

(d) any income earned by a legal entity fully or partially owned or controlled by a party, including income that is paid or assigned to any other party, person or legal entity;

(e) the party’s other financial resources;

(f) any trust:
(i) of which the party is the appointor or trustee; or
(ii) of which the party, the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is an eligible beneficiary as to capital or income; or

(iii) of which a corporation is an eligible beneficiary as to capital or income if the party, or the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is a shareholder or director of the corporation; or

(iv) over which the party has any direct or indirect power or control; or

(v) of which the party has the direct or indirect power to remove or appoint a trustee; or

(vi) of which the party has the power (whether subject to the concurrence of another person or not) to amend the terms; or

(vii) of which the party has the power to disapprove a proposed amendment of the terms or the appointment or removal of a trustee; or

(viii) over which a corporation has a power referred to in any of subparagraphs (iv) to (vii), if the party, the party’s child, spouse or de facto spouse is a director or shareholder of the corporation;

(g) any disposal of property (whether by sale, transfer, assignment or gift) made by the party, a legal entity referred to in paragraph (c), a corporation or a trust referred to in paragraph (f) that may affect, defeat or deplete a claim:

(i) in the 12 months immediately before the separation of the q parties; or

(ii) since the final separation of the parties;
(h) liabilities and contingent liabilities.
(3) Paragraph (2)(g) does not apply to a disposal of property made:
(a) with the consent or knowledge of the other party; or

(b) in the ordinary course of business.
(4) A party to a marriage or de facto relationship starting, or filing a response or reply to, a financial or property proceeding (other than by an Application for Consent Orders) must file, at the same time:
(a) a Financial Statement; and
(b) a financial questionnaire in accordance with the approved form, unless the party is required by these Rules to file an affidavit.
(5) If a party is aware that the completion of a Financial Statement will not fully discharge the duty to make full and frank disclosure, the party must also file an affidavit giving further particulars.

(6) If a party’s financial circumstances have changed significantly from the information set out in the Financial Statement or an affidavit filed under subrule (5), the party must, within 21 days after the change of circumstances, file:

(a) a new Financial Statement; or
(b) if the changes can be set out clearly in 300 words or less—an affidavit containing details about the party’s changed financial circumstances.
(7) Unless the court otherwise orders, a party (the first party) who is required by this rule to file a Financial Statement (other than a respondent to an application for maintenance only) must, before the first court date, serve on each other party who has an address for service in the proceeding the following documents:
(a) a copy of the party’s 3 most recent taxation returns;

(b) a copy of the party’s 3 most recent taxation assessments;

(c) if the first party is a member of a superannuation plan:
(i) the completed superannuation information form for any superannuation interest of the party (unless it has already been filed or exchanged); and

(ii) for a self‑managed superannuation fund—the trust deed and a copy of the 3 most recent Financial Statements for the fund;

(d) if the party has an Australian Business Number—a copy of the last 4 business activity statements lodged;

(e) if there is a partnership, trust or company (other than a public company) in which the party has an interest—a copy of the 3 most recent Financial Statements and the last 4 business activity statements lodged by the partnership, trust or company.

...
(9) This rule does not require a party to be served with a document that has already been provided to the party.
The obligation of full and frank disclosure to which parties to financial proceedings under the Act are subject and the importance of compliance with that obligation, have also repeatedly been emphasised in numerous authorities over many years.[7]
These proceedings have been plagued by persistent failures on the part of the husband to comply with his obligations pursuant to the Rules and court orders. These failures are discussed in the following paragraphs.
The consent orders imposed important obligations upon the husband with which he failed to comply, including the following:
(a) Order 18 of the consent orders required the husband to provide to the wife:
all “Form 524 Presentation of accounts and statement” lodged with ASIC in relation to [S Pty Ltd] since the husband left the partnership and within one month of any other such forms that are hereinafter lodged.
As noted by Berman J,[8] the contents of these documents was likely to be the only reliable indicator of the money received by the husband with respect to the S Partnership. Over transitional a period in 2017 and 2018, ASIC form 524 was replaced by forms 5602 and 5603, which required the same information. The husband opportunistically seized upon the change to the forms as an excuse to cease providing the relevant information to the wife. In this regard, Berman J found that the intention of paragraph 18 of the final orders, being that the wife would receive an ASIC document which would evidence the amount received by the husband, was frustrated by the husband ceasing to provide the equivalent ASIC document after 2018, the husband having “adopted the fiction” that because form 524 had been replaced by forms 5602 and 5603, there was no further obligation upon him to provide documents which would have confirmed his receipt of WIP.[9]

(b) Order 19 of the consent orders required the husband to authorise the wife’s access to documents and records of S Pty Ltd, including the statements of any bank account into which the husband received funds. Justice Berman found that the husband had failed to comply with this order, at least from November 2020.

(c) Order 21 of those orders required the husband to provide to the wife an annual statement of the gross and net funds received by him from S Pty Ltd. The husband did not comply with this order.

Further obligations were imposed on the husband following the commencement of the enforcement proceedings and have similarly not been complied with by him, as follows:
(a) As observed by the Full Court,[10] the enforcement application is a “financial proceeding” as defined in the Rules, and it therefore attracts the obligations contained in rules 6.01, 6.03 and 6.06, the contents of which are outlined above. Despite this fact and despite the wife having consistently sought disclosure from the husband of details of his financial circumstances and copies of documents relevant thereto, Berman J found that the husband had not complied with his obligations in this regard.

(b) On 21 August 2023, an order was made requiring the husband to file (inter alia) a Financial Statement. By 15 December 2023, the husband had not complied with that order. A further order was made on that day requiring (inter alia) that a Financial Statement be filed by him. He did not comply and yet another order was made on 14 February 2024 requiring the Financial Statement to be filed. The husband was ultimately relieved of the obligation to file a Financial Statement by order made on 31 May 2024, at which time it was determined that the issues ultimately determined by Berman J were to be determined by way of separate hearing.

(c) An order was made on 15 December 2023 requiring the parties to provide disclosure of information and documents relevant to the proceedings. A further order was made on 14 February 2024 requiring the parties to exchange disclosure, including but not limited to the documents set out in rule 6.06 of the Rules. The husband did not comply with these orders.

(d) The husband gave evidence that he had instructed an accounting firm to assist him with the reconciliation provided for at clause 10 of the Heads of Agreement. He conceded under cross-examination during the hearing before Berman J that a range of documents would either have been provided to the accounting firm by him or brought into existence by them arising from his instructions to them and that they had shown him a draft report they had been working on. No such documents were produced by the husband in these proceedings.[11]

As to the husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations, Berman J said as follows:
The evidence was replete with admissions by the respondent that there were documents available to him that had not been disclosed or provided. The respondent’s explanation was that he considered the application for enforcement to be without merit and on his case, a range of documents that would have enabled a better understanding of the WIP received, irrespective of how the respondent considered it should be treated, were not provided.
The husband failed to comply with his disclosure obligations to the extent that the wife, and as a consequence, Berman J, were left with no alternative but to do the best they could with incomplete evidence to calculate the WIP received by him for the purposes of the declaration ultimately made by Berman J.[12]
In the appeal, the husband sought to justify his failure to comply with his disclosure obligations by asserting that the materials sought by the wife were irrelevant because on the husband’s interpretation of order 17 of the consent orders, he had no liability to the wife. Unsurprisingly, this argument was roundly rejected by the Full Court.
The Full Court was satisfied that at the time of the listing of the appeal the husband was in breach of:[13]
(a) orders 18, 19 and 21 of the consent orders;

(b) orders 1 and 4 of the orders made on 15 December 2023;

(c) order 3 of the orders made on 14 February 2024; and

(d) the obligations cast upon him by rules 6.01(1), 6.03 and 6.06(3) of the Rules.

The Full Court further observed that the husband’s evidence in cross-examination and Berman J’s findings had left no room for doubt that these breaches had been deliberate,[14] and described them as “calculated, deliberate and significant.”[15]
The Full Court rejected an argument made on behalf of the husband that the obligations cast upon the husband had been brought to an end by Berman J’s judgment, observing that the duty of disclosure as outlined in rule 6.01continues until the proceeding is finalised (which had not yet occurred), and that the husband’s identified breaches were continuing and remained unremedied.[16]
The Full Court considered “egregious” to be “an apt description” of the husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations.[17] Their Honours further accepted “without reservation” a submission made on behalf of the wife that the husband’s breaches had directly interfered with the ascertainment of justice on the wife’s enforcement application,[18] observing that the husband’s breaches “had a direct impact upon the Court’s ability to properly consider and determine the issues before it [and were] likely to impede the Court’s ability to properly enforce its orders.”[19]
Further orders were made on 17 September 2025 requiring the husband (and the second respondent) to provide itemised disclosure by way of an affidavit of documents. These orders were not properly complied with, leading to the filing of the wife’s Application in a Proceeding on 18 November 2025 in which she sought to proceed on an undefended basis.

INTEREST

Relevant legal principles

The payment of interest on sums of money ordered to be paid is governed by section 117B of the Act, which provides as follows:
117B Interest on moneys ordered to be paid
(1) Subject to any order made by the court under subsection (2), where, in proceedings under this Act, a court makes an order for the payment of money (other than an order for the payment by way of maintenance of a periodic sum), interest is payable, at the rate prescribed by the applicable Rules of Court, from:
(a) the date on which the order is made; or

(b) the date on which the order takes effect;

whichever is later, on so much of the money as is from time to time unpaid.
(2) A court that makes an order for the payment of money as mentioned in subsection (1) may order that interest is not payable on the money payable under the first‑mentioned order or may order:
(a) that interest is payable at a rate specified in the order, being a rate other than the rate prescribed by the applicable Rules of Court; or

(b) that interest is payable from a date specified in the order, being a date other than the date from which the interest would be payable under subsection (1).

The rate of interest payable pursuant to subsection 117B(1) is set out in rule 10.17 of the Rules, which provides as follows:
Rate of interest

The prescribed rate at which interest is payable under paragraphs 87(11)(b), 90KA(b), 90UN(b) and subsection 117B(1) of the Family Law Act is:
(a) in respect of the period from 1 January to 30 June in any year--the rate that is 6% above the cash rate last published by the Reserve Bank of Australia before that period commenced; and

(b) in respect of the period from 1 July to 31 December in any year--the rate that is 6% above the cash rate last published by the Reserve Bank of Australia before that period commenced.

Note: For the date from which interest is payable, see paragraphs 87(11)(b), 90KA(b) and subsection 117B(1) of the Family Law Act.
Prior to 1 September 2021, the applicable rules were the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) (“the 2004 Rules”). The applicable rule was rule 17.03, which was at all material times identical to the current rule 10.17.
The effect of these Rule 10.17 (and previously rule 17.03 of the 2004 Rules) is and has at all material times been that the rate of interest payable is calculated by adding six per cent to the last Reserve Bank of Australia cash rate published prior to the prescribed period, the prescribed periods being 1 January to 30 June, and 1 July to 31 December of each year.

Terms of consent orders relating to interest

Subparagraph 8.3 of the consent orders provides that:
In the event of late payment the husband shall pay or cause to be paid to the wife interest on the amount outstanding from the date of default to the date of payment at the rate prescribed in the Family Law Rules 2004.
In my view, as submitted on behalf of the husband, subparagraph 8.3 refers to the payment of amounts referred to in subparagraph 8.2 (which imposed various payment obligations on the husband) and not to payments pursuant to other aspects of the consent orders, such as those the subject of the present application. However, this matters not, as it unnecessary to have recourse to the terms of subparagraph 8.3 in light of the operation of section 117B of the Act.

Has any part of the question of interest already been determined?

With respect to the question of interest, Berman J said the following:
The applicant also seeks an entitlement to interest pursuant to s 117B(1) of the Act.
The applicant argues that by the respondent ceasing to provide the updated ASIC documents to her in 2018 and the further decision to deny her access to the relevant accounts in 2020, interest should apply from at least 2020 if not 2018. Whilst egregious, the proceedings have been left to linger and it was open to the applicant to bring an enforcement application either in 2018 when the respondent ceased forwarding relevant ASIC documents and certainly in 2020 where he made it clear as set out in exhibit “12” [being an email sent by the husband to the wife] that he had no further obligation and the matter was effectively at an end.
...
The applicant seeks that there be declarations made pursuant to r 11.07(a) and that the proceedings then be listed for hearing as to the appropriate orders to be made that would enforce the amounts as declared. In circumstances where I have found that there be a declaration that the respondent pay to the applicant the sum of $724,426 and that her final member entitlement in the fund is $130,623, I consider it appropriate for the matter to be listed for hearing to determine the balance of the orders sought in the applicant’s Enforcement Application together with the application of default interest, if any.
On behalf of the husband, it was submitted that the wife’s application for interest was “an attempt to reopen issues already considered and disposed of by Berman J.” The husband submitted that by paragraphs [146] and [147], Berman J had addressed and rejected the wife’s claim to “pre-judgment interest” on the non-superannuation component (the WIP payment). He further submitted that Berman J had declared the amount of superannuation owed to the wife to be $130,623 notwithstanding that her case was advanced on the basis of that amount plus interest.[20]
The husband submitted that the comments made by Berman J at paragraph [147] expressly rejected the proposition of the wife that interest was payable, and that this foreclosed any claim for pre-judgment interest.
As a consequence, the husband submitted, the only question conceivably left open by Berman J’s judgment was whether default interest should accrue prospectively following the declaration of the non-superannuation debt, or alternatively, from the bringing of the enforcement application in 2023, being the point in time at which the wife’s delay, as identified by Berman J at [147], ceased to be a discretionary barrier.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that the husband’s submissions proceeded on the fundamentally wrong basis that the “judgment” in respect of which interest falls to be calculated is the 2025 orders of Berman J rather than the 2016 consent orders. Although I agree that such an interpretation would be incorrect, I do not accept that this is an accurate characterisation of the husband’s submissions.
I interpret the husband’s submissions not as mischaracterising Berman J’s judgment as having created the relevant payment obligations, but as interpreting his Honour’s judgment as having (in addition to declaring the amounts owing pursuant to the consent orders) expressly determined the issue of interest for the period between the payment obligations created by the consent orders falling due and the date of his Honour’s orders, with the expressions “pre-judgment” and “post-judgment” simply being convenient temporal descriptors. In case of any doubt, the obligations to which an entitlement to interest may or may not attach arose pursuant to the consent orders and existed prior to the determinations of Berman J (which quantified those existing obligations). Were it otherwise, any payer in default of a payment obligation could evade years of liability for interest simply by taking an oppositional position to interpretation of the order with which they had failed to comply, no matter how unmeritorious, and would be incentivised to do so.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that by paragraph [178], Berman J “expressly decided that the question of interest arising from the husband’s defaults was to be determined at a subsequent hearing.” The husband submitted that these words were prospective only and should be interpreted as contemplating, at most, the possibility of default interest accruing from the date of his Honour’s declarations (ie 7 February 2025). The wife submitted that the husband’s position that the issue of interest has already been decided “flies in the face of the express words of Berman J reserving that issue to further hearing.”
I accept the submission made on behalf of the wife that Berman J did not address the wife’s position with respect to the date from which interest was payable because his Honour expressly determined to stand that issue over for further hearing.
In my view, had his Honour determined that a temporal limitation was to imposed upon the question of any interest payable by the husband with respect to the obligation contained in order 17 of the consent orders, this would have been reflected in his Honour’s orders and reasons for judgment.
The conclusion that his Honour did not conclusively determine the question of interest is consistent with the husband’s own lack of certainty as to whether Berman J’s words should be interpreted as precluding interest from the date of his Honour’s orders or from the date upon which the wife filed her enforcement application. It is difficult to see how his Honour’s observations could be taken as precluding the payment of interest after the date upon which that application was filed, even if it had been his Honour’s intention to preclude the payment of interest during a period of delay on the part of the wife, but the more significant issue is that the husband’s own resort to guesswork in this regard is consistent with the issue not having been conclusively determined.
Whilst his Honour certainly expressed some reservations at [147] with respect to the wife not having acted promptly in bringing her enforcement application, in my view, these observations constituted obiter dicta. I do not accept that his Honour’s observations in this regard indicate that his Honour determined any part of the question of the interest (if any) that would accrue pursuant to section 117B or otherwise. Rather, as submitted by the wife, his Honour expressly decided and ordered that such a determination would be left to a later date.
With respect to the superannuation split, the husband submitted that Berman J’s declaration that the wife’s entitlement in the fund was $130,623 was definitive as to her entitlement at that point, such that the declared figure was “final and exhaustive” and thereby precluded the recovery of interest. I do not accept this submission. Had Berman J intended to extinguish the wife’s entitlement to interest over and above the base amount, which entitlement was expressly contained within subparagraph 36.1 of the consent orders, it would be expected that his Honour’s orders and reasons would both expressly have said so. Neither did.
Order 36 was not varied so as to remove the entitlement to interest expressly contained therein. As discussed later in these reasons, such a variation would have required an application under section 79A of the Act.[21] No such application was made to his Honour and accordingly, his Honour did not address the requirements of that section. I do not accept that that entitlement has been extinguished.
Nor do I consider that his Honour’s orders or reasons can be interpreted as constituting a determination that the discretion to enforce the interest entitlement created by that order (and/or by subsection 117B(1)) should not be exercised. In my view, properly construed, his Honour’s declaration with respect to superannuation dealt only with the quantum of the base amount and left the wife’s entitlement to interest thereon untouched.
It was not necessary for his Honour’s declarations as to the amounts owed to the wife pursuant to orders 17 and 36 of the consent orders to specify that interest was payable upon those sums in order to make it so. Nor was the wife required to make out or even express a “claim” for interest. Indeed, the reverse is true. The operation of subsection 117B(1) and the express provision conferring an entitlement to interest contained within order 36 operate such that the wife already had such an entitlement.[22] If that entitlement was to be removed or reduced, such removal or reduction was required to be expressly ordered, which did not occur.
I do not accept that any part of the question of the interest payable with respect to the obligations created by orders 17 and 36 of the consent orders was determined by Berman J.

Is there a discretion as to whether interest accrues or the rate of interest payable?

It was submitted on behalf of the wife that the imposition of interest pursuant to subsection 117B(1) is not discretionary.
In this regard, the wife relied upon the judgment of Cronin J in Wohen & Wohen,[23] where his Honour said: “[i]t is quite clear, when you read that particular provision, that there is no need for court intervention and interest automatically applies on the particular judgment [subject to subsection 117B(2)].” Similarly, Finn J (sitting as a member of the Full Court) in Matthews & Matthews (“Matthews”)[24] observed that “the right to interest in respect of monies due and unpaid under an order made under the Act for the payment of money, is a right conferred by the Act itself...”
Subsection 117B(2) clearly confers a discretion with respect to the payment of interest, including as to whether interest is payable, the rate at which interest is payable, and the date from which interest is payable. What is less clear form the terms of the subsection itself is whether that discretion can only be exercised at the time of the making of the order for the payment of money (in this case, on 22 July 2016, when the consent orders were made), or whether it can be exercised at a later time, such as the present.
In Matthews, Finn J expressly left open the question of whether an order under subsection 117B(2) varying the operation of subsection 117B(1) can be made at any time other than the time at which the order for the payment of money referred to in subsection 117B(1) is made.[25]
In Stephens & Stephens and Anor (Enforcement) (“Stephens”),[26] the Full Court held as follows:
In our view, where a property settlement order provides for the payment of money, then in the absence of an order to the contrary, interest is payable at the rate prescribed by the Rules from the date on which the order is made, or the date on which the order takes effect, whichever is later, on so much of the money as is from time to time unpaid. However, the court that made the property settlement order may order that interest is not payable or may order that interest is payable at a rate specified in the order, being a rate different from the rate prescribed by the Rules, or that interest is payable from a date specified in the order. The Wife was entitled as from the date of the judgment of Strickland J to the benefit of s 117B(1) of the Act and the Husband did not appeal against that outcome.
In our view, the discretion conferred by s 117B(2) of the Act is to be exercised by the court that made the order for the payment of money and cannot be exercised by any other court.
...
Strickland J made an order that on or before 28 February 2006 the Husband pay to the Wife the sum of $2,182,302.00. His Honour did not make an order for the payment of interest. However, the Husband failed to pay the amount due and therefore pursuant to s 117B(1) of the Act as and from 28 February 2006 the Husband had to also pay to the Wife interest at the rate prescribed by r 17.03 of the Rules.
In the applications that were for hearing before Strickland J neither party sought an order pursuant to s 117B(2) of the Act. However, the Wife sought an order, namely, “[s]uch further or consequential orders as may be necessary to effect and implement a just and equitable settlement of property issues between the parties”. The Husband sought an order, namely, “[s]uch further or consequential orders as are appropriate to effect a just and equitable settlement of property issues between the parties”.
Strickland J could have determined pursuant to s 117B(2) of the Act that in the event that the amount of $2,182,302.00 was not paid by 28 February 2006 that interest was not payable or that interest was payable at a rate specified by his Honour, being a rate other than the rate prescribed by r 17.03 of the Rules, or that interest was payable from a date specified by his Honour being a date other than 28 February 2006. However, given that his Honour did not exercise the discretion conferred by s 117B(2) then s 117B(1) applied. At the relevant time the prescribed rate of interest was 10.25 per cent. The judgment at first instance was affirmed on appeal and hence the interest will run from the date of the judgment below.
...
In our view the appeals in relation to interest can be disposed of shortly. The Wife was entitled by reason of paragraph 4 of the property settlement order of Strickland J to the benefit of interest in accordance with s 117B(1) of the Act as from the date by which the Husband was to pay the money to the Wife. On a plain reading of s 117B any exercise of the discretion conferred by s 117B(2) was a matter for Strickland J. Strickland J was not asked to exercise the discretion conferred by s 117B(2) and thus Coleman J should not have exercised that discretion. In the result the appeal by the Wife should be allowed and the appeal by the Husband dismissed. ...
The husband in Stephens unsuccessfully sought special leave to appeal to the High Court.[27] In the course of determining that application, Heydon J said as follows:
The fourth special leave question is whether “the power under s.117B(2) of the Family Law Act may only be exercised by the Judge making the order for payment of money referred to in s.117B(1).” The fifth special leave question is whether “the approach to the selection of a rate of interest payable under s.117B(2) taken by the primary Judge was inappropriate.” Whatever the answer to these questions, no injustice in the outcome in relation to the rate of interest selected in the Full Court has been demonstrated.
In Elrod & Elrod,[28] Riethmuller J confirmed, by reference to Stephens, that there is no discretion pursuant to section 117B of the Act to vary the amount of interest payable after the final orders have been made. His Honour said as follows:
Whilst s 117B(2) of the Act makes provisions for the Court to order that interest is not payable or is payable at a different rate other than what is specified in the relevant rules, the wording of the section indicates that such orders must be made at the time of making the original order. To find otherwise would undermine the finality of the orders (see Stephens & Stephens & Anor (Enforcement) [2009] FamCAFC 240; (2009) FLC 93-425). The purpose of the rate of interest set out in the Rules was explained in Perot v Perot [2013] FamCA 137 at [27], where it was said that:
The rate set in the Rules, by the body of Judges, is not simply a compensatory rate. It is also a rate aimed at providing some level of encouragement to litigants to comply with orders that require them to pay money within certain periods. Adopting the same public policy underpinning, namely the encouragement of litigants to comply with money orders made by the court, the rate selected is uniform across most superior courts.
The wife submitted that the husband ought to have filed a formal application for an exercise of the discretion under subsection 117B(2) if he sought a departure from the application of interest as provided for in subsection 117B(1). The husband submitted that the Court, in determining the present application, was already seised of that issue, and that the power to make an order relating to interest was part of the exercise of power undertaken by Berman J. In my view, the upshot of the authorities outlined above is that neither position is entirely correct. The appropriate time for any application and any exercise of discretion with respect to the payment of interest is neither now nor at the time of the determination undertaken by Berman J, but in 2016 when the consent orders were made.
I do not consider that there remains any discretion pursuant to subsection 117B(2) to vary or dispense with the entitlement to interest which is conferred upon the wife by subsection 117B(1).

To the extent that there is a discretion, how should it be exercised?

Despite the discretion in subsection 117B(2) no longer being available, enforcement of an obligation to pay interest is almost certainly discretionary by virtue of the discretionary nature of the enforcement power in section 105 of the Act.[29] Section 105 relevantly provides as follows:
105 Enforcement generally

(1) Subject to this Part, to the regulations and to the applicable Rules of Court, all decrees made under this Act may be enforced by any court having jurisdiction under this Act.

...
Whilst the terms of subsection 105(1) refer specifically to enforcement of “decrees,” it is likely that enforcement of an obligation to pay interest arising upon the making of an order constitutes an aspect of enforcement of that order. Indeed, the discretion under section 105 has been recognised in a number of cases regarding the payment of interest.[30] Furthermore, it is indisputable that enforcement of the interest obligation expressly provided for in order 36 of the consent orders would constitute enforcement of a decree and would therefore attract the discretion incorporated in section 105.[31]
Furthermore, whilst as the law presently stands, I am bound by the Full Court’s decision in Stephens, I have regard to the fact that the members of the Full Court in Stephens themselves considered the possibility that their interpretation may not be correct,[32] and that subsection 117B(2) has been interpreted in a number of cases as permitting the discretion to be exercised at a time later than the time at which the order imposing the obligation to pay money has been imposed.[33]
As a consequence, I will consider whether the discretion not to enforce the interest entitlements attaching to the orders in question should be exercised; and in an abundance of caution, I will also consider whether, to the extent that there remains any discretion to order that interest is not payable or to vary the rate of interest or the date from which such interest should be payable, that discretion should be exercised such as to effect such a variation to the wife’s existing entitlement to interest.
Returning to the issue raised on behalf of the wife regarding the lack of a formal application made by the husband with respect to interest, I do not accept that such an application is required in the current circumstances. In my view, the orders made on 8 December 2025 permit the ventilation of issues relating to the question of interest by way of written submissions without the need for any further formal application and there can be no question that the wife has had proper notice of what it is that the husband seeks in this regard.
As to the question of the exercise of the discretion not to enforce an order, the relevant authorities require consideration of whether the party seeking that the order not be enforced has established that it would be inequitable to enforce it, with what is inequitable to be determined by notions of general fairness.[34] Prima facie, a party is entitled to the benefit of a court order that has been made in their favour.[35]
In the context of the exercise of discretion with respect to enforcement of an obligation to pay interest, in Chiodo & Shaw,[36] Ryan J said as follows:
...I see no reason to decline to enforce the interest component due in relation to the property settlement orders. Firstly, the applicant provided no evidence as to why in the interests of justice the interest rate prescribed in the Rules should not be enforced. Next, the purpose of interest is not merely compensatory and is imposed at a rate designed to ensure compliance with orders. As their Honours in Stephens explained a commercially low rate of interest may result in a recalcitrant party liable to pay an amount of money taking advantage of an interest rate which is less than the prescribed rate. ...
Similarly, in Dewell & Harris,[37] Rees J held that the onus lay with the payer to demonstrate that it was in the interests of justice not to enforce an obligation to pay interest in accordance with the rate provided for in the Rules. The husband in that case had not discharged that onus in circumstances in which he had kept and invested the funds and potentially earned income from them and had deprived the wife of the same opportunity.
In Arlington & Buzzotti,[38] Hogan J declined to exercise the discretion not to enforce an obligation to pay interest, saying “I am simply not persuaded that, in the circumstances which have arisen since the order was made, it is inequitable to enforce this obligation.”
As to the exercise of the discretion contained within subsection 117B(2), the Full Court in Stephens gave consideration, in the event that their Honours’ determination that the rate of interest could only be varied at the time of the making of the order imposing the payment obligation was wrong, to the considerations applicable to the exercise of that discretion. Their Honours said as follows:
Section 117B(2) allows the court, by order, to vary some of the consequences which otherwise flow automatically from s 117B(1), namely by ordering that no interest be paid or by varying the rate, or the date from which it becomes payable. The wording suggests that s 117B(2) offers a divergence from the effect of s 117B(1) and thus limits its operation by working as an exception. We are also of the view that as an exception, s 117B(2) shifts the onus onto the party seeking to take advantage of the exception: see Rural Export & Trading (WA) Pty Ltd v Hahneuser [2007] FCA 1535; (2007) 243 ALR 356 per Gray ACJ at [65].
The discretion conferred by s 117B(2) of the Act is extremely wide. However, in our view it “enables such lower or higher rate of interest to be determined as the interests of justice in a particular case may require”: see Gould and Another v Vaggelas and Others [[1985] HCA 75; (1985) 157 CLR 215].
We would not seek to prescribe the considerations that may be relevant to the exercise of the discretion conferred by s 117B(2) of the Act. We are of the view that a purpose of interest on a judgment debt is to compensate the party entitled to the benefit of the judgment until it is satisfied. However, we do not accept that this is the only purpose and are of the view that another purpose is to ensure compliance with orders. For example, there may be circumstances where a recalcitrant party liable to pay an amount of money may seek to take advantage of an interest rate which is less than the prescribed rate.
...
The Wife was entitled to the benefit of the rate prescribed in the Rules and she sought enforcement of the Husband’s obligation to pay an amount so calculated. It was submitted on behalf of the Wife that, “the proceedings were in effect proceedings for enforcement of the order made by Strickland J on 30 November 2005”. There was no obligation on the Wife to put evidence before the Court directed to establishing why it would be in the interests of justice to retain the prescribed rate. We accept the submissions by senior counsel for the Wife that there was no onus on the Wife to establish that the rate prescribed by the Rules would in the circumstances adequately compensate the Wife. In any event, as senior counsel for the Husband pointed out it would be very difficult “to prove what is compensatory” ...
The Husband in his Response sought that the Wife’s application be dismissed and he provided no evidence as to why in the interests of justice an order should be made that the interest be at a rate lower than that prescribed in the Rules. However, after the discussion with the trial Judge on 17 February 2009 the Husband with leave put on evidence as to a rate of interest lower than that prescribed in the Rules. However, again the Husband provided no evidence as to why in the interests of justice the interest rate should be varied to a rate lower than that prescribed in the Rules.

...
The trial Judge was obliged to be satisfied that it was in the interests of justice that the interest rate be varied ...
In this case, it is the husband who bears the onus of demonstrating either that it would be in the interests of justice to vary or dispense with the requirement for the payment of interest, or that it would be inequitable to enforce that aspect of the orders.
Save to the extent that he submitted that Berman J had determined the question of interest adversely to the wife as a consequence of delay on her part in bringing the enforcement application (a proposition I have rejected), the husband did not submit that any delay on the part of the wife should lead to the exercise of the discretion not to enforce the interest-related components of the consent orders. For the sake of completeness however, I shall consider this issue.
Delay has been recognised as a factor relevant to the exercise of discretion to enforce an order.[39] In Yilmaz & Yilmaz,[40] Le Poer Trench said:
Failure to press for enforcement of a court order is a matter which, in my view, could justify the court in exercising its discretion not to enforce an order requiring interest to be paid on the unpaid portion of the principal sum during the period the order was not actively being pursued. ...
I note, however, that the wife’s evidence is that between 2 June 2018 (at or around the time the husband ceased complying with the order relating to the ASIC forms) and 17 April 2023 (shortly prior to the filing of the enforcement application), she contacted the husband (both directly and through his solicitors) on no fewer than 16 occasions regarding his failure to comply with the orders. In such circumstances, it cannot be said that the wife was doing nothing with respect to enforcement of her entitlements, and the husband cannot reasonably have been under any misapprehension that she acquiesced in his breaches or accepted his assertions as to the proper interpretation of the consent orders.
Furthermore, I note that Berman J recorded[41] that the husband had not, prior to the hearing before his Honour, advised the wife of the true nature of his position that the reconciliation and finalisation of S Partnership was a condition precedent to any payment to her, and indeed, had presented her with a different position.
The husband was under an ongoing obligation to keep the wife properly informed of relevant facts and circumstances after the making of the consent orders and he wilfully failed to do so. This misconduct on the part of the husband plainly presented difficulties for the wife and her advisors with respect to the determination of whether and when enforcement proceedings should be brought and hindered her ability to make an informed decision in this regard.
Moreover, there is no evidence before the Court to suggest, and nor was it submitted, that the delay in the commencement of the enforcement proceedings caused any prejudice to the husband.
In such circumstances, I do not accept either that the timing of the wife’s application renders it inequitable for the husband’s obligation to pay interest to be enforced in full, or that the delay renders it in the interests of justice to vary the requirement for the payment of interest for the period to 8 December 2025.
No other basis for satisfaction of the onus borne by the husband in this regard was advanced on his behalf, and nor have I identified any. To the contrary, the evidence before the Court as to the husband’s blatant disregard of his obligations suggests that failure to enforce them in full would be to reward the husband’s poor conduct and thereby visit injustice upon the wife.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that interest should be payable in circumstances in which the husband had enjoyed the benefit of the full amount he had withheld from the wife from her entitlements pursuant to the consent orders, with the result that the wife had been deprived of the use of the funds for investment or other purposes. It was further submitted that the husband should not gain the windfall of the use of the sums of $724,426 in non-superannuation funds and $130,623 in superannuation at the expense of the wife from his own deliberate and ongoing breaches of the orders. I accept those submissions.
The husband has not discharged the onus of demonstrating that it would be contrary to the interests of justice or inequitable for the wife’s entitlement to interest pursuant to subsection 117B(1) and (in the case of the superannuation splitting order) the express terms of the consent orders, to be enforced in full and without alteration. Neither the discretion as to enforcement nor any remaining discretion as to the quantum of interest payable shall be exercised in the husband’s favour.

Calculation of interest

WIP payments

Subsection 117B(1) provides that interest is payable from the later of the date upon which the order is made, or the date on which the order takes effect.
The calculation of interest with respect to the payments due to the wife pursuant to order 17 of the consent orders is complicated by a combination of the fact that the husband’s payment obligations accrued over time (in accordance with both the receipt by him of fees referrable to S Pty Ltd WIP and the payment of the liabilities provided for in order 17) and the husband’s recalcitrance with respect to his disclosure obligations. This complexity was described by senior counsel for the wife as being:
that the precise timing of the husband’s receipt of WIP funds together with the time at which any netting-off of allowable deductions is both extremely complicated (because the husband received an enormous number of payments of WIP over time) and impossible to determine with precision (because the husband has never made proper disclosure of documents in relation to the recovery of WIP by him or the payment of expenses by him).
As submitted on behalf of the wife, neither the wife nor the Court are in a position to calculate with precision what payments were due by the husband to the wife from time to time between the making of the consent orders in 2016 and the hearing of the application before Berman J in 2024, at which time the wife satisfied the Court that payments to her totalling $724,426 were outstanding.
The wife’s proposal for dealing with this difficulty entailed ascertaining, to the best of her ability on the available evidence (limited as it has been by the husband’s non-disclosure), a date range within which various components of the total sum of $724,426 must have fallen due, and using, for the purposes of specifying the time at which interest commenced accruing on each component of the husband’s payment obligations, the midpoint of each relevant period (thereby treating the each component as having accrued evenly across the relevant period).
In circumstances where the husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations similarly precluded precise determination of the dates upon which payments of GST and the payments referred to in subparagraphs 17.1-17.4 of the consent orders had been made, the wife proposed that the percentage of the total fees received that was represented by each component be calculated, and that that percentage be applied to the total net sum payable by the wife for the purposes of the interest calculations.
In order to ascertain the applicable date ranges, the wife relied on the following evidence, which had been accepted by Berman J as being the best available evidence:
(a) WIP was recovered into a “Westpac CIT Account” from approximately April 2014 to approximately April 2015 in the sum of $295,110.13;[42]

(b) WIP was recovered into a “NAB WIP Account” from approximately April 2015 to approximately November 2020 in the sum of $1,361,664.38;[43]

(c) other sums not recorded in the above bank accounts totalling $37,099.35 were recovered prior to August 2018;[44]

(d) other sums not recorded in the above bank accounts totalling $92,575.07 were recovered after August 2018 but prior to the hearing commencing in July 2024;[45]

(e) the husband asserted to the wife that “collection of the WIP of the S Partnership has been completed” on 20 November 2020;[46] and

(f) the husband asserted at trial that the recovery of WIP may have ceased by June 2018 but “certainly by 2020.”[47]
