King’s counsel for the husband submitted that the Court should not adopt “speculative methodologies to manufacture due dates or interest accrual in the absence of factual findings as to timing and quantum.” It was submitted in this regard that the approach promoted by the wife “substitutes arithmetic assumption for factual findings that were not made” and was inconsistent with the approach adopted by Berman J, which fixed a single aggregate sum owing.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that the husband’s criticism of the methodology adopted by her constituted no more than an attempt by him to profit from his own breaches of orders and the Rules. I accept that submission.
As submitted on behalf of the wife, there is extensive and longstanding authority[48] supporting the propositions that:
(a) where there is a lack of candour, the Court should err on the side of the party disadvantaged by that lack of candour;

(b) the Court should not be unduly cautious in making findings in favour of the other party where a party fails to make proper disclosure;

(c) where there is non-disclosure, the Court must do the best it can with the limited information available to it; and

(d) the Court may, where it is possible to do so, extrapolate from the information that is available to it, combined with appropriate assumptions, in favour of the innocent party to draw conclusions.

These principles were in part referred to and applied by Berman J,[49] and the Court’s extreme disapproval of the husband’s non-compliance with his disclosure obligations could not have been made clearer by the Full Court’s judgment. If the husband was at any time under any misapprehension as to the likelihood that his poor conduct with respect to his disclosure obligations would result in negative consequences for him, there could not conceivably have been any lingering uncertainty following the Full Court’s judgment. Yet the husband has remained obdurate and his non-compliance has not been rectified.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that the husband cannot be heard to complain about a lack of precision in the calculation of interest when it is his own ongoing and egregious breaches of orders (and the Rules) which prevents greater precision. I agree.
It was not only open to the husband but incumbent upon him to adduce such evidence as would enable the wife and the Court to make more precise calculations than those proposed by the wife. He elected not to do so. Nor did he proffer any proposal for an alternative method of calculation.
The Court’s inability to assess the interest payable by the husband with greater precision than is set out in the proposal of the wife is a circumstance entirely of the husband’s own making. In those circumstances, the balance of risk of detriment arising from the lack of precision should favour the wife and not the husband. I am satisfied that the calculation methodology proposed by the wife presents a fair and appropriate means of calculating the interest owed to her by the husband in the circumstances presented by the husband’s recalcitrance.
Senior counsel for the wife produced detailed schedules[50] setting out the calculation of the interest payable to her using her at the rate provided for in the Rules (as applicable from time to time). No issue was taken with those calculations, nor have I been able to identify any error therein.
The interest payable by the husband to the wife with respect to the obligation imposed by order 17 of the consent orders for the period to 8 December 2025 (which is to be paid in accordance with subparagraph 5.6.1(c) of the orders made on 8 December 2025) is assessed at the sum of $487,229.94.

Superannuation

With respect to the superannuation split provided for at order 36 of the consent orders, subparagraph 36.1 provided that the amount to be paid to the wife was to be calculated using a base amount of $130,632 “plus 6% calculated from 18 November 2015 to the date of the rollover referred to in paragraph 36.7.3...”.
Subparagraph 36.7.3 provided that the wife “as soon as practicable after 30/06/2018 then rollover her member entitlements into a Superannuation Fund of her choice.” That obligation was expressed (at the commencement of subparagraph 36.7) to arise “[f]orthwith upon the preceding orders 36.1 to 36.5 having been carried out in full,” which did not occur because of the position taken by and inaction on the part of the husband with respect to the superannuation order.
The wife’s primary position was that pursuant to subparagraph 36.1 she was entitled to interest at six per cent from 18 November 2015 to 30 June 2018, and interest pursuant to section 117B thereafter, which she calculated to be a total of $110,874.08.
In the alternative, the wife sought interest at the rate of six per cent (being the rate provided for at subparagraph 36.1 of the consent orders) for the entire period, being a total interest amount calculated by her to be of $78,889.13.
The husband submitted that Berman J’s declaration was not an order for the payment of money by the husband personally and therefore did not attract the operation of subsection 117B(1). It is in fact order 36 of the consent orders, and not Berman J’s declaration, which imposes the relevant obligation to pay money. Berman J’s declaration quantified the obligation but did not create it.
In Jones & Jones (“Jones”),[51] the Full Court considered the meaning of the phrase “an order for the payment of money” in section 117B. Their Honours held as follows:
We are of opinion that on its proper construction section 117(B) [sic] when it refers to “an order for payment of money” prima facie means an order for the payment of money by one party to the other. Ordinarily it would not refer to a situation such as the one here, where jointly owned property is to be realized by the parties and where the proceeds of such realization are to be distributed between the parties. In the present case each of the parties was required by the order to join in the sale of the home and the distribution of the proceeds. To order the husband to pay interest on monies payable to the wife, the bulk of which had to be raised by the wife herself out of her own share in the home, was quite unjust and not a result which was intended by section 117(B) [sic].
There may be cases where one party has the sole use of the property to the exclusion of the other until a sale takes place and in such a case it might be appropriate to order the payment of interest or to make some other adjustment pending the completion of the sale. Similarly, it might be appropriate to order the payment of interest where one party unreasonably delays or frustrates the ordered sale. However, this was not such a case. In our opinion the order for the payment of interest by the husband to the wife cannot be justified.
In my view, whilst a superannuation splitting order might similarly not ordinarily be considered an order “for the payment of money,” the husband’s conduct in the present proceedings may well justify a departure from the general approach of the type referred to by the Full Court in Jones.
The fact that the payer pursuant to order 36 of the consent orders is not the husband in his personal capacity is immaterial. There is nothing in the terms of subsection 117B(1) to suggest that its application is limited to orders directed against spouses (or other payers) personally.[52] The effect of subparagraph 7.2 of the orders made on 8 December 2025 is that any interest payable with respect to order 36 of the consent orders will be paid from the same source as the underlying obligation, being the superannuation fund. It is not suggested that the requirement to pay interest will be visited directly upon the husband in his personal capacity.
In any event, in my view, the reference in subparagraph 36.1 of the consent orders to interest at the specified rate of six per cent being payable “from 18 November 2015 to the date of the rollover” should be interpreted as meaning that the interest rate specified in that order applies until the rollover actually occurs, and not merely until the date upon which the rollover was expected to occur. This is particularly so in circumstances in which the time specified in the orders for the implementation of the rollover was not a precise date, but “as soon as practicable after” the date referred to therein.
In Carchar & Hbenum,[53] the parties had fixed a rate of interest as part of consent orders made nearly 20 years prior to the conclusion of the enforcement proceedings. Cronin J said as follows:
It was common ground that enforcement of the order was a discretionary matter. The range of options open include dismissing the application, staying the application and enforcing the order on terms and conditions. The power to enforce orders lies in s 105(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
Enforcing the order but imposing terms and conditions sounds simple but once the jurisdiction in s 79 is exhausted, save as to the confined circumstances in s 79A, a review of a decision of a delegated officer or an appeal, the Court cannot alter the substantive entitlements of the parties (see Mullane (1983) FLC 91-373). Any suggestion that the capital payment or the 18 per cent interest rate could be altered would require a finding that the order or part of it to be altered was a machinery provision rather than a substantive entitlement of the parties. As explained in Ravasini and Ravasini (1983) FLC 93-312, the Court has power to vary machinery or consequential provisions of a property order. However, the power to vary machinery provisions is limited and can only be implemented to allow the intention of the order to be effected. In varying such a provision, a court must have regard to issues of justice and equity between the parties. The test of whether a provision is consequential or substantive is whether the change concerns a part of the substantive order or whether it is simply a change to give efficacy to the substantive order.
In this case, there is no power for me to alter the provisions of the order by way of a term or condition without affecting the substantive right of the wife and ultimately, the husband.
...
There was debate as to what the appropriate sum was. This is not a case in which there is any room for doubt arising from the provisions of s 117B of the Act. The parties chose in 1991 to fix the rate of interest rather than leave the provisions of the Act to apply. For the reasons I earlier outlined about that being a substantive entitlement, there is no power for the Court to alter it notwithstanding the substantially different interest regime that now applies. (see also the discussion in Stephens and Stephens and Anor (Enforcement) [2009] FamCAFC 240 at 413 and the subsequent comment of the High Court in the special leave application).
In my view, the same observations apply in the present matter, and the Court retains no discretion with respect to the interest rate provided for in subparagraph 36.1 of the consent orders (other than the discretion to decline to enforce, which I have already discussed).
As a consequence, a declaration will be made in accordance with the wife’s alternative position that interest applies at the rate of six per cent (being the rate provided for at subparagraph 36.1 of the consent orders) for the period 18 November 2018 to 8 December 2025. I calculate this as being interest in the amount of $78,889.14, being one cent higher than the amount calculated by the wife, and will accordingly order interest in that amount.

COSTS

The wife sought her costs of the enforcement proceedings, including the ancillary applications, on an indemnity basis, or in the alternative, on a party/party basis in accordance with the scale set out at schedule 3 of the Rules.
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that the only application for costs before the Court was the wife’s application filed on 28 February 2025 following the delivery of Berman J’s judgment. As with the similar argument put against the husband with respect to interest, I consider that the orders made on 8 December 2025 permit the ventilation of all issues relating to the question of costs without the need for any further formal application. In any event, the wife’s enforcement application itself, her Initiating Application filed on 2 September 2025, and her Application in a Proceeding filed on 18 November 2025 all contained applications for costs.

Relevant legal principles

Determination of applications as to costs in proceedings under the Act is governed by section 114UB of the Act. Prior to the commencement of the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) on 10 June 2025, costs applications were governed by section 117 of the Act. The relevant transitional provision[54] with respect to the amendments relating to costs provided as follows:
The amendments relating to costs made by Divisions 1 and 2 of this Part apply in relation to:

...
(a) proceedings instituted before, and not finally determined before, 10 June 2025, other than proceedings where a final hearing has commenced.
Which of the costs-related provisions is to be applied therefore depends on whether this was a proceeding “where a final hearing ha[d] commenced” as at 10 June 2025. That, in turn, depends upon whether the hearing before Berman J, which commenced in on 24 July 2024, was “a final hearing.”
The orders of 31 May 2024 referred to the hearing listed before his Honour as “a separate hearing” and as an “interim defended hearing.” The orders of 7 August 2024 referred to the continuation of that hearing having been listed for “trial” on a date which was vacated, and listed the “all applications for final orders” for “hearing” on 9 December 2024, though it is noted that the matter remained at all material times listed for separate hearing on discrete issues as provided for in the orders of 31 May 2024. Cross-examination was undertaken at the hearing, which is a feature ordinarily (but not exclusively) characteristic of final hearings.
In my view, in circumstances in which it was at all times known to the parties and to the Court that the hearing before Berman J (which was ultimately heard in two tranches) would not finalise the enforcement proceedings, it was not, properly characterised, a “final hearing.” If I am wrong in this interpretation, however, my consideration of the relevant factors and the outcome of the application will be unaffected because the applicable provisions are identical in all material respects to the formerly applicable provisions of section 117.
The relevant parts of section 114UB provide as follows:
114UB Costs
(1) Subject to subsection (2) of this section, subsection 102QAB(6) and sections 114UD and 114UE, each party to proceedings under this Act is to bear the party’s own costs.

(2) If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable Rules of Court, make any order for costs and security for costs that the court considers just, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.

(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:

(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer; and

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

The general rule pursuant to subsection 114UB(1) is that each party must bear that party’s own costs. Subsection 114UB(2) provides that if the Court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the Court may depart from that general rule. As the High Court made clear in Penfold & Penfold,[55] the general rule in subsection (1) is not paramount to subsection (2) and must yield whenever a judge finds in a particular case that there are circumstances justifying the making of an order for costs. Subsection (2) requires a finding of justifying circumstances as an “essential preliminary” to the making of an order. Beyond this there is nothing in the subject matter or in the interrelationship of the two provisions which imposes any additional or special onus on an applicant for an order for costs.[56] The Court holds a broad discretion with respect to orders for costs.[57]
Considerations relevant to departure from the general rule are set out in subsection 114UB(3). No single factor is determinative and it is not necessary that more than one of these factors be satisfied in order for a costs order to be made.[58] It is only necessary to address those factors which are relevant to the matter at hand.[59] Those relevant to this matter are considered in the following paragraphs.

The financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings

The husband’s persistent and ongoing recalcitrance with respect to his disclosure obligations limits the ability of the Court to ascertain his financial circumstances with any degree of certainty. The uncertainty as to his financial circumstances includes, but is not limited to, the extent of the WIP which Berman J found was “likely” to have been received by him after 2020,[60] 60 per cent of the net value of which ought to have been paid to the wife. As submitted by the wife, it also includes lack of disclosure as to how he applied the large sums of money received by him from S Pty Ltd (including both the funds he was entitled to retain for his own benefit and those he was not). For the reasons already discussed, the uncertainty as to the husband’s financial circumstances arising from the husband’s contemptuous conduct will not be interpreted in his favour.
Senior counsel for the wife pointed to the fact that the husband has had the full benefit of the use of the funds which ought to have been paid to the wife, though this fact will be addressed by the order for interest which is to be made. The husband has also had the full benefit of his own 40 per cent share of the WIP received by him, which, even if limited to the amount disclosed by him, has been a substantial total sum.
The wife also emphasised that the husband has had the use of the former matrimonial home which was transferred to him pursuant to the consent orders (noting that his financial circumstances permitted him to transfer a 50 per cent interest in that property to his current wife for nil consideration). This is accurate, though I note that that property is to be sold and the proceeds applied to payment of the husband’s extant obligations to the wife pursuant to the orders made on 8 December 2025.
The wife otherwise points to her evidence of entities under the control of the husband through which he holds other real property holdings, including a holiday house which has been renovated and which is used to generate income.
A Financial Statement ultimately filed by the husband indicates that he earns a gross income $190,008 per annum. It discloses significant expenses, but I note that those expenses include superannuation contributions which would appear to be voluntary in circumstances in which the husband is not an employee, and mortgage payments with respect to the former matrimonial home, being an expense of which the husband will soon be relieved. The husband will also soon be relieved of liability for the mortgage encumbering the former matrimonial home, being the major liability disclosed by him. Some of the other liabilities asserted by the husband are contingent in nature, being in the form of guarantees. The husband will retain the balance of the proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home after the wife’s entitlements pursuant to the orders of the Court are paid to her.
The husband’s Financial Statement disclosed a liability of $99,902 relating to legal fees. The wife submitted, and I accept, that it cannot count in the husband’s favour that he chose to waste money on futile appeals from Berman J’s reasons. I would add to that that the husband’s expenditure on his own legal fees for the enforcement proceedings themselves, his position throughout which took the form of a futile attempt to avoid meeting his obligations pursuant to the consent orders, to the extent that this has affected his current financial circumstances, ought not count in his favour when it comes to the question of costs payable to the wife.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife, and I accept, that the husband could not be heard to submit that he had limited assets or income in circumstances in which it had been found that he had failed to make proper disclosure in relation to his financial circumstances and where he had continually and wilfully prevented any proper understanding of his financial affairs. I give limited weight to the husband’s evidence of his financial circumstances in light of the serious and ongoing deficiencies with respect to his compliance with his disclosure obligations.
The wife has deposed to residing in a property which was formerly wholly owned by her parents, a one-third interest in which has been transferred to her by them for no consideration. She has no other assets of substantial value. She earns a gross income of just under $110,000 per annum. The wife’s Financial Statement discloses that she owes a debt to her parents of approximately $420,000 pursuant to a personal loan.
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that his financial circumstances are not favourable compared to those of the wife. I could not possibly accept such a proposition as accurate in the absence of full disclosure. In any event, it is not a precondition to an order for costs that there be a disparity in the parties’ financial circumstances[61] or that the party against whom costs are sought be in financial circumstances more favourable than those of the party seeking costs.[62]

The conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

The conduct relevant to this consideration is conduct as a litigant.[63] The relevant authorities are replete with examples of the kinds of conduct which will ordinarily justify an order for costs and which are representative of the kinds of conduct in which the husband in these proceedings has persistently engaged. Those examples include obstructiveness causing the proceedings to be unduly prolonged or made unduly expensive to the other party,[64] failure to make proper information available (either in affidavits or documents) or being obstructive in the collection of relevant material,[65] and failure to provide proper disclosure as to a party’s financial circumstances.[66] It must, however, be borne in mind that the purpose of a costs order is to compensate the person in whose favour it is made, not to punish the person against whom the order is made.[67]
Consistently with the applicable authorities, rule 6.17 of the Rules outlines the potential consequences of non-disclosure and expressly warns that if a party does not disclose a document as required by the Rules (as has been the case of the husband in the present proceedings with respect to numerous documents), that party may be ordered to pay costs.[68] As identified by Keane J in Rozenblit v Vainer,[69] litigation conducted in “sheer disregard of the rules” is oppressive to the other party.
I have discussed the husband’s failures to comply with his disclosure obligations and the various other obligations imposed on him by orders of the Court, together with the observations of both Berman J and the Full Court in this regard, at length earlier in these reasons. Notwithstanding the clear indications contained in those judgments, the husband’s recalcitrant behaviour has persisted.
In addition to the conduct already discussed, the wife points to failures on the part of the husband to comply with procedural directions including filing documents in response to the enforcement application over 15 weeks late, failing to file a Response to an Initiating Application, failing to file a summary of argument when directed to do so, and repeatedly failing to file costs notices.
The husband also failed to keep the wife and the Court properly informed of his position in the proceedings. For example, as Berman J recorded, the husband had failed to inform the wife (and plainly therefore also the Court) prior to the commencement of the hearing before his Honour of his position that the reconciliation of WIP was a condition precedent which had not yet taken place.[70]
The husband attempted to justify his continued failure to comply with the consent orders following the delivery of Berman J’s judgment on the basis that he was exercising his appellate rights, and on the basis that he did not wish to sell the former matrimonial home before determination of the Full Court appeal or the application for special leave. In the absence of a stay, neither of these reasons constitutes any kind of justification whatsoever for his ongoing failure to comply with binding court orders. Rather, this approach is simply emblematic of the husband’s ongoing high-handed attitude towards orders of the Court, which takes the form of a belief that his own wishes and desires should be placed ahead of the need to comply with binding orders. Plainly, it is not for a litigant to determine the time at which they will choose to comply with orders that are binding upon them according to their wishes.
There can be no doubt that the costs incurred by the wife have been impacted by the need to address the husband’s ongoing non-disclosure, to meet his unmeritorious excuses for his failure to comply with his obligations, and to continue to pursue her entitlements in the face of the husband’s resistance to acceptance of binding obligations imposed upon him.
Senior counsel for the wife submitted, and I accept, that the husband’s conduct in connection with the enforcement proceeding had gone beyond the unreasonable and uncommercial, though I observe that it was undoubtedly both of those things. The husband is a sophisticated and experienced litigant. He has been legally represented throughout these proceedings, including by King’s counsel. He has also had the benefit of two separate judgments expressly denouncing his serious and unacceptable conduct with respect to the proceedings. There can be no suggestion that his ongoing contumelious and egregious behaviour has been anything other than a knowing and wilful disregard for his obligations and for the authority of the Court.
The husband referred to Berman J’s observation that the proceedings had been “left to linger” and submitted that it had been open to the wife to bring the proceedings at an earlier time. Assuming that submission were accepted, it is not clear how that goes to the question of costs, as there is no evidence or even suggestion that the proceedings would have been less costly to either of the parties had they been commenced earlier in time.
The husband otherwise scrambled to point to perceived transgressions in the manner in which the wife had presented her case, alleging a failure on the wife’s behalf to disclose to the Court communications made to her by the husband of assertions which were ultimately rejected (including that no monies were owed to her), and criticising her for refusing to participate in mediation despite the fact that she already had binding orders in her favour.
The husband also complained that it was not until the commencement of the hearing before Berman J that he had become aware of the amount claimed against him. This complaint entirely fails to grapple with the fact that it was the husband, and not the wife, who was subject to a binding obligation to provide disclosure of the very information that would have revealed the amount owed by him to the wife. As submitted by senior counsel for the wife, the Court and the Full Court have unequivocally said that it was as a result of the husband’s breaches of court orders that the wife was unable to quantify her claim. I note also that it was the husband, and not the wife, who was found by Berman J to have failed to make his position clear prior to the hearing before his Honour.[71]
The triviality of the transgressions alleged by the husband (even if any such minor wrongdoing on behalf of the wife had in fact been demonstrated, which it has not), stands in stark contrast to the overwhelming and persistent recalcitrance of the husband. His attempt to malign the conduct of the wife, who has diligently prosecuted her application, serves only to throw his own egregious misconduct into sharp relief.
The husband further asserted that the adjournment application made by the wife, which led to a part-heard adjournment during which she had intended to file an application pursuant to section 79A of the Act but had not ultimately done so, had increased the costs of the hearing before Berman J. It is not at all apparent that this assertion is accurate, but in any event, it is clear from Berman J’s judgment that the basis for that adjournment application and the source of the need for the wife and her advisors to consider a section 79A application, was evidence given by the husband indicating that he had not discovered and produced documents relevant to the issues in the proceedings.[72] In those circumstances, pointing to lost costs arising as a consequence hardly assists the husband.
Furthermore, as the wife submits, in circumstances in which the entire enforcement proceeding arose from the husband’s blatant breaches of orders of the court, the wife should not be criticised for simply seeking to enforce her rights and running her case as carefully as she could in the face of the “shifting sands” of the husband’s position and his ongoing lack of disclosure.
The husband’s submissions with respect to this factor conspicuously largely ignored his own egregious conduct with respect to the conduct of the proceedings. This observation is by no means a criticism of the author of those submissions. This simply reflects the inescapable reality that the husband’s conduct is indefensible.
I give significant weight to the husband’s persistent egregious and recalcitrant conduct when considering the question of costs.

Whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court

This is a significant factor in the circumstances of this case. It must be borne in mind that this was an enforcement proceeding. From its very inception, the wife had the benefit of binding court orders in her favour, which should have brought the proceedings between the parties to an end nearly 10 years ago, but with which the husband failed to comply.
There can be no doubt that the entirety of the enforcement proceeding (including the various ancillary applications) has been necessitated by the husband’s non-compliance with various obligations imposed upon him by the consent orders. The husband accepted this reality but emphasised that he had held the view that no funds were owed to the wife. Reiterating his incorrect and unmeritorious position does not assist the husband.
Senior counsel for the wife submitted as follows:
Litigation between the wife and husband should have concluded with the making of the 2016 orders. All of the parties’ extensive legal costs in relation to the current Enforcement Application arise directly and exclusively from the husband’s fundamental and unjustified breaches of the 2016 orders, compounded by his brazen breaches of the Rules and orders of this Court in relation to the application.

Had the husband simply paid the amounts required as and when they fell due and permitted the wife to roll out her adjusted member balance in the [Catlin Superannuation Fund] there would have been no cause for any further proceedings. Instead of complying with his obligations the husband has delayed and obfuscated for nine years. His attitude to compliance with the Court’s orders is typified by the fact that without any justification whatsoever he has simply failed to pay the amounts declared to be due by Berman J on 7 February 2025 for 11 months. Even now, the wife has been forced to incur costs in relation to enforcement and the husband’s transfer of property out of his own hands when the husband could simply have complied with his obligations at any time.

The husband’s primary justification, such as it is, for his ongoing breaches of the Court’s orders was held to be “not a credible argument”.
I accept these submissions in their entirety.

Whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings

The husband was wholly unsuccessful before Berman J, who rejected the husband’s interpretation of the consent orders and made declarations in the terms sought by the wife.
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that the wife’s Initiating Application filed on 16 August 2024 had been wholly unsuccessful. I do not agree. Although that application was dismissed, it merely represented the wife’s alternative position in the event that the husband succeeded with respect to the primary issue of interpretation of the consent orders. As submitted on behalf of the wife, when the husband’s arguments failed in their entirety, her alternative case fell away with them. The dismissal of that application in such circumstances is indicative of success on the part of the wife, not the husband.
In any event, this factor deals with the case where a party is wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings themselves, not with respect to specific issues or applications.[73]
It is true, as pointed out on behalf of the husband, that what remained of the enforcement proceeding was ultimately resolved by consent on 8 December 2025. However, it is of significance that the consent orders made on 8 December 2025 reflected the outcome sought by the wife and effectively represented capitulation on the part of the husband. Furthermore, the husband’s consent to those orders came only after several years of costly litigation in which he sought unsuccessfully to challenge the underlying obligations, causing the wife to incur the costs now under consideration, and occurred only at the very end of the proceedings in circumstances in which he was faced with an application to proceed undefended. The making of an order by consent does not, in any event, preclude an order for costs.[74]
The wife has succeeded in relation to her alternative position rather than her primary position with respect to the interest on the superannuation component of the consent orders, but the husband was wholly unsuccessful with respect to his position that there should be no interest payable.
As Finn J (with whom O’Ryan and Ainslie-Wallace JJ agreed) said in Davida & Davida (Costs):[75]
...True it is that the relevant paragraph in section [114UB(3)] refers to a party being “wholly unsuccessful”, but I think it fair to say that the practice has been to look at what one might term the relative merits of success or lack of success between the parties, even if necessary doing that under the last matter mentioned in section [114UB(3)], being any “other” matter.
Whether it be said that the husband has been wholly unsuccessful within the meaning of subsection 114UB(3)(e) or almost wholly unsuccessful such that the applicable subsection is 114UB(3)(g), the wife has been overwhelmingly successful when compared to the husband and that is a significant consideration in the context of this matter.

Overarching purpose

I have regard to the overarching purpose as set out in section 95 of the Act, which (relevantly) provides as follows:
95 Overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions
(1) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes:
...
(c) according to law; and

(d) as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.

Note: For family law practice and procedure provisions, see subsection (4).
(2) Without limiting subsection (1), the overarching purpose includes the following objectives in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the just determination of all such proceedings;

(b) the efficient use of the judicial and administrative resources available for the purposes of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(c) the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(d) the disposal of all such proceedings in a timely manner;

(e) the resolution of disputes at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.

(3) The family law practice and procedure provisions must be interpreted and applied, and any power conferred or duty imposed by them (including the power to make applicable Rules of Court) must be exercised or carried out, in the way that best promotes the overarching purpose.

(4) The family law practice and procedure provisions are the following, so far as they apply in relation to proceedings under this Act:

(a) the applicable Rules of Court;
(b) any other provision made by or under this Act, or any other Act, with respect to the practice and procedure of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia or any other court.
Section 96 of the Act requires parties to act consistently with the overarching purpose. The conduct on the part of the husband which I have already discussed plainly constitutes direct breaches of his obligations in this regard. It is well-established that breaches of the overarching purpose (including the equivalent provisions found at sections 37M and 37N of the Federal Court of Australia Act 1976 (Cth)) are to be taken seriously and can justify orders for costs.[76]

Conclusion as to whether costs order justified

In light of these considerations, I am satisfied that there are circumstances which justify the making of an order for costs.

Basis of assessment of costs

The wife seeks that the order for costs be made on an indemnity basis, or in the alternative, on a party/party basis. The husband proposed that if there were to be an order for costs, the quantum of costs be assessed.
The ordinary rule is that where the Court makes an order for costs, the order is for the payment of those costs in accordance with the scale as set out in Schedule 3 to the Rules.
There is nothing in the Act which inhibits an order for indemnity costs.[77] The power to award indemnity costs is in fact expressly provided for at subrule 12.17(1)(b) of the Rules.[78] If indemnity costs are to be ordered, however, exceptional circumstances are required, as set out by the Full Court in Kohan & Kohan (“Kohan”)[79] and other authorities including, in particular, Prantage & Prantage (“Prantage”).[80]
The husband emphasised that the Full Court in Kohan held that “the purpose of fixing a scale of costs must be understood to signify that they contain the normal rates of charges.” I accept that that is a very important consideration, and I give it due regard.
It cannot be doubted that an order for indemnity costs represents “a very great departure from the normal standard,”[81] and that “the Court should not depart lightly from the ordinary rules relating to costs between party and party and the circumstances justifying the departure should be of an exceptional kind.” [82] There should be “some special or unusual feature in the case to justify the court in departing from the ordinary practice”[83] and “indemnity costs should only be awarded in an “exceedingly rare situation.”[84] The question then, is whether such exceptional circumstances exist in the present case.
The husband referred to the Full Court’s decision in Munday & Bowman,[85] in which Holden CJ drew from the well-known decision in Colgate-Palmolive Company & Anor v Cussons Pty Ltd (“Colgate-Palmolive”),[86] in providing examples of circumstances that may warrant the exercise of discretion to award indemnity costs. It was submitted on his behalf that those examples did not apply to the present matter. In my view, at least two of the examples provided do in fact apply to the circumstances of the present case, including:
(a) Where it appears that an action has been commenced or continued in circumstances where a party properly advised should have known that they had no chance of success; such that the action must be presumed to have been commenced or continued for some ulterior motive or because of some wilful disregard of the known facts.[87] In this regard, I accept the submission made on behalf of the wife that properly advised, the husband should have made disclosure and paid what he owed promptly. I further accept the submission that, noting that the husband was represented, including by King’s counsel, and there is no suggestion (by the wife or by the Court) that his representatives failed to comply with their obligations as officers of the court, that the husband’s dogged refusal to comply with his obligations to the court arises from an unreasonable failure by the husband to heed the advice he was given and can only have been driven by ulterior motives. One such possible motive consistent with the manner in which the husband conducted his case would be to prevent the Court from doing justice as between the parties, but it is not necessary to make a finding in this regard.

(b) Evidence of particular misconduct causing loss of time to the court and to other parties.[88] Even putting aside the fact that the whole of the enforcement proceeding arose from the husband’s failure to abide by the consent orders, the husband’s wilful misconduct, including but not limited to his failure to comply with his disclosure obligations, caused extensive loss of time to the Court and to the wife. Both the Court and the wife were forced to do the best they could to work around the husband’s concealment of evidence, which “directly interfered with the ascertainment of justice.”[89] I accept the submission made on behalf of the wife that the husband’s misconduct both caused and greatly prolonged the enforcement proceedings.

In any event, the examples of conduct likely to warrant an order for indemnity costs as outlined in Colgate-Palmolive are merely examples. They do not represent an exhaustive list, and as Sheppard J confirmed in that case, the categories in which the discretion may be exercised are not closed and it is not a condition precedent to the exercise of the discretion that some collateral purpose or species of fraud be established.[90]
In Ruhani v Director of Police,[91] Kirby J, drawing on established authorities, described circumstances in which departure from the usual award of party/party costs might be justified. Several of the examples given apply to the circumstances of the present case, including where the opponent's conduct has been “plainly unreasonable,” undertaken in an “unmeritorious, deliberate or high-handed” way or where that opponent has been shown to be guilty of “unreasonable conduct, albeit that it need not rise as high as vexation.”
Wilful disregard for a party’s disclosure obligations, “sheer disregard for the rules”[92] and failure to comply with the overarching purpose have all led or contributed to the making of orders for indemnity costs in past cases.[93] All are features which have not only been present in this case but have actively undermined the ability of the Court to do justice as between the parties. In my view, this case falls within the recognised category of cases in which an indemnity costs order may properly be made to compensate a successful party for the consequences of delinquency on the part of an unsuccessful party.[94]
It was submitted on behalf of the husband that although his interpretation of the consent orders had ultimately been found to be wrong, his position had been bona fide. It was further submitted that it had given rise to “arguable factual and legal questions”[95] and had not been “doomed to fail” or a hopeless case. The husband’s position was not simply found to be “wrong.” His position with respect to WIP was found not to be credible,[96] and his argument with respect to superannuation was described by the Full Court as “particularly weak.”[97]
Furthermore, any prospect the husband may otherwise have had of satisfying the Court that there was any merit in his case or even that the basis upon which he was pursuing it was bona fide, was comprehensively stymied by the husband’s own conduct in failing to comply with his obligations and thereby facilitate comprehensive evidence being put before the Court. The manner in which the husband conducted his case undermines the suggestion that it was pursued bona fide.
The husband submitted that the adjournment application made by the wife during the hearing before Berman J and the Initiating Application ultimately filed by her on 16 August 2024 indicated that she herself had identified potential flaws in her enforcement application. Senior counsel for the wife disputed this, pointing out that the wife had merely responded to the position advanced by the husband by putting forward an alternative position; and that her primary position, which has been fully vindicated, was at all times that the consent orders were clear and enforceable. I accept that submission and do not accept that there were any identified “potential flaws” in the wife’s ultimately wholly successful enforcement application. Comprehensively and diligently taking all reasonably available steps to meet all aspects of the case put by the other is appropriate and proportionate conduct on the part of a litigant. It does not signify that, and cannot be taken as a concession that, the case of the other has merit.
The husband relied on Stephens & Stephens and Anor (Enforcement) (Costs) (“Stephens (Costs)”)[98] as being analogous to the facts of the present matter, noting that that case involved an unsuccessful appeal from a first instance decision on an enforcement application and had also involved an unsuccessful application for special leave. Indemnity costs were not ordered in that case. However, this decision can be distinguished on at least two bases. Firstly, it was a judgment with respect to the costs of an appeal, not with respect to the underlying enforcement proceedings. Not only does this mean that it was an application with respect to a fundamentally different stage of the proceeding, but it has the consequence that the conduct of the husband in that matter with respect to the underlying application was not canvased in detail in the judgment, limiting the extent to which the circumstances of the two cases can be compared. Secondly, the husband’s non-compliance with his obligations in the present matter was so serious and so egregious that the Full Court declined to allow him to prosecute his appeal at all, which circumstance was not a feature of Stephens (Costs).
Moreover, while the Full Court in Stephens (Costs) was not satisfied that the husband’s conduct in those proceedings warranted an indemnity costs order in the appeal, their Honours expressly indicated that this did not preclude such conduct being raised with respect to a pending costs application in relation to the underlying enforcement proceedings.[99]
In my view, an example of a more analogous case is LGM & CAM,[100] in which an appeal against an order for indemnity costs was unsuccessful (notwithstanding the fact that the underlying finding of contempt had in fact been overturned on appeal) in circumstances where it was found that the appellant’s conduct had justified the enforcement proceedings, where the Full Court was satisfied that it could be inferred that had such proceedings not been taken, the appellant would have continued to be in breach of the Court’s orders, and where the Full Court held that any order other than indemnity costs would, in the circumstances, have resulted in the respondent unfairly being visited with the costs of proceedings, the necessity for which rested and remained with the appellant.
The wife in the current proceedings annexed to her written submissions as to costs detailed schedules of the costs sought be her, calculated both on an indemnity basis and on a party/party basis. A copy of the costs agreement entered into between the wife and her solicitors was annexed to her affidavit filed on 28 February 2025.[101]
The husband took issue with some relatively minor aspects of the calculation of the party/party costs claimed by the wife. I note also that although the schedule of party/party costs relied on by the wife appears to reflect changes in the amounts provided for in the scale pursuant to amendments to the Rules made from time to time over the relevant period, it does not appear to reflect the fact that from 11 July to 15 December 2023, these proceedings were pending in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2). That court has its own separate scale of costs,[102] though rule 4.01 of the rules of that court[103] does allow for the application of the scale applicable in this court to family law proceedings pending in that court, and this may well be a matter in which it that course would be appropriate.
Despite these issues, the schedules relied upon by the wife allow at least a general comparison between the applicable party/party costs and the indemnity costs sought. The total amounts sought by the wife were $145,176.85 in party/party costs and $190,594.39 in indemnity costs. Even allowing for the potential need for some variation to account for the identified issues with respect to the calculation of party/party costs, the difference between these sums is not, in my view, sufficient to weigh significantly against the making of an order for indemnity costs.[104]
In Prantage,[105] in considering the importance of the increasing disparity between scale costs and the costs being charged by lawyers, the Full Court referred to the judgment of Cooper and Merkel JJ of the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia in Re Wilcox, Ex parte Venture Industries Pty Ltd, (“Re Wilcox”)[106] in which their Honours identified the “two seemingly irreconcilable objectives” of “relieving a successful litigant from the burden of costs which that litigant should not have been required to incur” and “protecting access to justice by only exposing an unsuccessful litigant in the usual course to an order for scale costs on a party and party basis.”
In my view, in the present matter, not only is it of importance that there is not in fact a significant disparity between the scale costs and the costs charged by the wife’s lawyers, but the husband is not merely an “unsuccessful litigant” but also a recalcitrant litigant whose ongoing breaches of orders and other obligations have been held to have been “calculated, deliberate and significant.”[107] In such circumstances, there is less cause for concern than would ordinarily be the case about the latter of the objectives identified in Re Wilcox, and greater weight can be given to the former.
Had the husband merely failed to persuade Berman J and the Full Court of his interpretation of the consent orders and otherwise complied in full with his obligations as a litigant, this would not likely be a case where indemnity costs would be warranted. That, however, is not what occurred, and a comparison to such a scenario is entirely inapt. It is the husband’s ceaseless and wilful misconduct, and not merely his lack of success (comprehensive as it was), that marks the present matter as exceptional.
Save for some minor adjustments as discussed below, I have not identified anything apparently unnecessary, unreasonable or disproportionate with respect to the work undertaken by, or the rates charged by, the wife’s lawyers as represented in the schedule relied upon by her with respect to her application for indemnity costs which would weigh against an order for the costs she seeks.[108] I am satisfied that the costs charged to the wife, and now sought from the husband, have been appropriate,[109] particularly having regard to the significant forensic challenges that have been faced by the wife and her legal representatives as a consequence of the husband’s conduct.
A costs notice filed on behalf of the husband on 5 December 2025 indicates that his own costs and disbursements for the same proceeding have been slightly higher than those claimed by the wife (even allowing for lack of clarity in that notice as to the extent to which outstanding counsel’s fees relate to the enforcement proceedings as distinct from the appeal and/or the special leave application). This enhances the ability of the Court to be satisfied as to the reasonableness, appropriateness and proportionality of the costs claimed by the wife.
A further relevant consideration with respect to an order for indemnity costs is the likely impact on the financial position of each of the parties and the actual financial significance for them of a departure from the usual approach.[110] I refer in this regard to my earlier observations with respect to the financial circumstances of the parties and note again that the husband’s failure to comply with his disclosure obligations has created a circumstance where the Court is entirely unable to conclude that there is any realistic prospect of an unjust impact upon him of making the order sought by the wife.
It was submitted on behalf of the wife that in the circumstances of this case, she should not be required to remain out of pocket to any degree whatsoever for her reasonable legal costs, which arise exclusively from the husband’s breaches of multiple orders and persistent and unjustified refusal to accept the reality of his position. I accept that submission. If the wife is left responsible for the payment of legal costs in enforcing the orders by which she was entitled to the receipt of funds, the value of those orders is diminished by the sum of legal fees paid by her. In the circumstances of this case, to allow that to occur would occasion injustice upon the wife.
I am satisfied for the reasons outlined above that this falls into the category of “exceptional” cases as referred to in the relevant authorities in which an order for indemnity costs is both justified and just.

Costs to be paid

The husband took issue with the fact that the costs sought by the wife dated back to 2019 in circumstances in which the enforcement proceeding was not commenced until 2023. Senior counsel for the wife submitted that this was “hardly surprising” in circumstances in which the husband had been in breach of his obligations since 2016. I agree. No particular issue was otherwise raised by the husband with respect to the specific costs sought by the wife to the extent that they were sought on an indemnity basis.
Orders made on 31 May 2024 and 11 July 2025 provided that there would not be orders for costs of the hearings on those days. The costs sought by the wife appropriately reflected the terms of those orders.
Several of the items claimed in the wife’s schedule as to indemnity costs assumed that the hearing listed for 8 December 2025 would proceed for the two days for which it was listed, when in fact the matter resolved (save for the issues dealt with in this judgment) on 8 December 2025, with the result that no hearing day took place on 9 December 2025.
This included solicitors’ work for the trial (item 260 of the solicitors’ work section of the wife’s schedule of indemnity costs), in the sum of $5,600 excluding GST ($6,160 including GST); and counsel’s hearing fee (item 5 of the counsel section of the wife’s schedule of indemnity costs) in the sum of $9,500 excluding GST ($10,450 including GST). Half of each of these amounts (a total of $8,305 including GST) will be deducted from the amount sought by the wife to reflect the fact that the hearing proceeded only for one day. A daily hearing fee for the second hearing day of $1,070 (to which GST does not apply) was included in the schedule but was also not ultimately payable, and that will also be excluded.
As to the husband’s application that should any costs be ordered, the quantum be assessed, it was submitted on behalf of the wife that the Court should be slow indeed to subject the wife to yet further delay and disputation from husband. Having regard to my conclusions above as to the reasonableness of the costs sought by the wife, and the relevant authorities outlining the circumstances in which it is appropriate that costs be fixed rather than referred for assessment,[111] I accept that submission.
I am otherwise satisfied that it is just to make the costs order as sought by the wife and will order that the husband pay the wife’s costs in the sum of $181,219.39.

CONCLUSION

For all of the reasons outlined above, I make the orders and declarations as set out at the commencement of these reasons.
